results.title: TreeSeeker: Tree-Structured Trial, Error, and Return in Deep Search
results.content:
TreeSeeker addresses this by organizing search as branch-and-return exploration over tree-structured states. Our controller, TreeSearch, uses textual signals of value, uncertainty, and risk to decide whether to exploit a promising branch, explore an uncertain alternative, or prune and return from an unproductive continuation. TreeMem keeps branch-local evidence, conflicts, and failure cues so later decisions can be informed by earlier trials.

Across XBench-DeepSearch, BrowseComp, and BrowseComp-ZH, TreeSeeker consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines. We hope this work contributes to building more reliable and adaptive research agents.

This is an automated message from the Librarian Bot. I found the following papers similar to this paper. [...] trees, identifies active goals, and uses textual UCB signals of value, uncertainty, and risk to select among exploiting a promising branch, exploring an uncertain alternative, or pruning an unproductive continuation and returning to an earlier branch point. TreeMem supports this control loop by keeping evidence, uncertainty, conflicts, progress, and failure cues attached to the branches that produced them, so trial outcomes can guide later decisions. Experiments on XBench-DeepSearch, BrowseComp, and BrowseComp-ZH show that TreeSeeker consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines, suggesting that explicit branch-and-return control complements stronger reasoning and tool execution. [...] Deep search requires agents to answer complex questions through multi-step web search, browsing, evidence comparison, and synthesis. A central challenge is deciding how to search when several directions look plausible but only some will later lead to reliable evidence. If an agent greedily follows the current best-looking direction, it may keep extending a weak continuation. If it explores without discipline, it may waste budget on disconnected trials. We propose TreeSeeker, an inference-time framework for controlled trial-and-error in deep search. TreeSeeker organizes search as branch-and-return search over tree-structured states, where each branch is a tentative direction for a sub-goal. At each round, TreeSearch reads all sub-goal trees, identifies active goals, and uses textual UCB

# Consider alternatives to tree-sitter #41

@eyelidlessness

## Description

@eyelidlessness

I’m filing quickly on mobile ahead of our check in, but briefly:

I briefly explored Ohm over the weekend and it’s a pretty compelling alternative option (albeit with some reservations around maturity). Beyond its TS support, I was encouraged by how easy it was to build a cromulent XPath grammar, and how much more closely it matches the grammar from the spec.

results.title: Best Tree Service Software 2026: Top 7 Tools Compared (ROI Analysis)
results.content:
The platform is deliberately simple—which is exactly what makes it work for smaller teams. Your office manager can master it in under an hour, and crew members need just 10 minutes of training on the mobile app.

ROI Factor: At $49/month for the basic plan, you break even by saving just 1 hour of administrative time monthly. Most users report saving 8-12 hours weekly.

### 5. WorkWave: The Routing Specialist

Best for: Companies with 5+ trucks prioritizing route efficiency and fuel savings

Pricing: $79-$150/month per user

WorkWave’s claim to fame is routing optimization that borders on magical. The system considers traffic patterns, crew capabilities, equipment location, and time windows to build routes that consistently save 15-20% on drive time.

Key Features: [...] Pricing: $69-$349/month (14-day free trial)

Jobber isn’t tree-specific, but its intuitive interface and robust mobile capabilities make it the top choice for companies tired of complicated software their team won’t actually use. The learning curve is under 2 hours—your entire team can be operational by lunch.

Key Features:

 Quote-to-cash in under 5 minutes: Create estimates, get approval, schedule work, and collect payment without leaving the platform
 Client hub portal: Customers approve quotes, pay invoices, and book services 24/7
 Two-way text messaging: Built-in SMS keeps all communication in one place
 GPS time tracking: See exactly when crews arrive and leave job sites
 QuickBooks integration: Automatic sync eliminates double-entry bookkeeping [...] The platform excels at follow-up automation. You can set triggers like “send estimate reminder after 3 days” or “request review 24 hours after job completion”—the kind of consistent follow-up that increases close rates by 34% but rarely happens with manual systems.

ROI Factor: The automated payment reminders alone reduce average days-to-payment from 28 to 11 days. For a company with $50K monthly revenue, that’s a $27,000 improvement in working capital.

### 3. ServiceTitan: The Enterprise Solution

Best for: Large operations ($2M+) with multiple crews, complex routing, and call center needs

Pricing: Custom pricing (typically $600-$1,200+/month for tree services)
